finding.vuln_claim: `dir` is concatenated into the SQL `ORDER BY` clause without validation, enabling SQL injection.
This endpoint is an unauthenticated SSRF proxy because it fetches any user-supplied URL with axios.get().
The single `replace('../', '')` check is bypassable, so a crafted `file` can still escape `/srv/data` via path traversal.
`pickle.loads` deserializes attacker-controlled cookie data, enabling code execution or object injection.
The route updates any user by `:id` without verifying ownership or admin rights.
`Object.assign(user, req.body)` allows mass assignment of protected fields.
Untrusted `name` is interpolated into a shell command, allowing command injection.
The `src` prefix check does not stop shell expansion, so injected shell syntax still executes.
User-supplied Markdown is rendered to HTML and stored without sanitization, enabling stored XSS.
The balance check and two writes are not atomic, so concurrent transfers can both pass the check and overspend the same source account.
In production, any token that fails signature verification is still accepted via `jwt.decode`, so attackers can forge arbitrary claims.
Direct `==` comparison on the HMAC leaks signature length/prefix timing.
The `startswith('/')` check still allows `//evil.com`, which browsers treat as an external redirect target.
Untrusted XML is parsed with entity resolution and network access enabled, allowing XXE and SSRF via external entities.
User-controlled `url` is fetched server-side with `requests.get`, allowing SSRF to internal or cloud-only endpoints.
`gets(buf)` reads unbounded input into a 64-byte stack buffer, allowing a stack buffer overflow.
The access token is stored in SharedPreferences as plaintext.
The AES key is hardcoded in source, so anyone who reads the code can decrypt all ciphertexts and forge encrypted data.
The null-check is inverted, so a valid `out` pointer is rejected while `NULL` can reach `memcpy` and `out[n]`.
`dir` is interpolated directly into the `ORDER BY` clause, enabling SQL injection.
This endpoint fetches attacker-controlled URLs, creating server-side request forgery.
The target check is malformed and cannot reliably block partner API URLs.
Single replace of '../' lets attackers keep traversal segments and escape `/srv/data`.
Untrusted cookie data is base64-decoded and passed to `pickle.loads`, enabling arbitrary code execution via crafted `state` cookies.
Untrusted `name` and `src` are interpolated into a shell command and executed with `os.system`, enabling command injection.
Untrusted comment body is converted to HTML and stored without sanitization, enabling stored XSS.
Negative `amount` values are accepted, letting an attacker increase their own balance and debit the target account.
In production, any token that fails signature verification is still accepted via `jwt.decode`, bypassing authentication.
The signature check uses a normal equality comparison, which can leak HMAC bytes via timing.
Scheme-relative URLs like `//evil.com` pass the check and become an open redirect.
The XML parser resolves external entities, allowing XXE when attacker-supplied XML is parsed.
The login gate is hardcoded to `true`, so authentication does not actually verify credentials.
This endpoint fetches attacker-supplied URLs server-side, enabling SSRF to internal hosts and cloud metadata.
`gets(buf)` can overflow the 64-byte stack buffer with unbounded input.
The access token is stored in plaintext SharedPreferences, so a local attacker, backup extract, or rooted device can recover it.
The AES key is hardcoded in source, so anyone with code or a leaked artifact can decrypt all ciphertexts.
The guard is inverted, so valid `out` pointers are rejected and a NULL `out` reaches `memcpy` and `out[n]`.
`direction` is concatenated into the ORDER BY clause without validation, enabling SQL injection.
This endpoint fetches any user-supplied URL with axios, enabling SSRF to internal or metadata hosts.
A single `../` strip is bypassable, letting an attacker read arbitrary files outside `/srv/data` via crafted traversal input.
User-controlled cookie data is base64-decoded and passed to `pickle.loads`, enabling arbitrary code execution via crafted pickle payloads.
The handler blindly copies req.body into a user record, so attackers can overwrite protected fields like role or isSupport.
The endpoint updates any user by URL id without checking ownership or admin rights, enabling IDOR writes.
User-controlled `name` and `src` are interpolated into a shell command and executed with `os.system`.
User-controlled Markdown is rendered to HTML and stored without sanitization, enabling stored XSS.
A negative `amount` is accepted and turns the debit into a credit, letting the sender mint funds.
The balance check and updates are non-atomic, so concurrent transfers can overspend the same balance.
Production falls back to `jwt.decode(t)`, so any malformed or unsigned token can be treated as authenticated data without verification.
The HMAC check uses `==`, which can leak signature validity through timing differences.
Protocol-relative targets like `//evil.com` pass the slash check and redirect users off-site.
The XML parser resolves external entities, allowing XXE and possible SSRF or local file disclosure from attacker-controlled XML.
Untrusted body fields are used directly in a Mongo-style query, allowing NoSQL operator injection.
The endpoint fetches an attacker-controlled URL server-side, enabling SSRF to internal services and metadata endpoints.
`gets(buf)` can overflow the 64-byte stack buffer because it reads unbounded input.
The AES key is hardcoded in source, so anyone who reads the code can decrypt all ciphertexts.
The null check is inverted, so a NULL `out` with nonzero `out_sz` reaches `memcpy` and crashes.
Untrusted `direction` is interpolated into SQL, enabling ORDER BY injection.
This is an SSRF proxy because user-controlled `url` is fetched directly with `axios.get`.
Single replace only strips one `../`, so a payload like `../../etc/passwd` still escapes `/srv/data`.
Attacker-controlled cookie is deserialized with `pickle.loads`, enabling remote code execution.
Broken authorization check can let non-support users reach the update path.
Untrusted `name` is interpolated into a shell command and executed with `os.system`.
Untrusted comment Markdown is rendered to HTML and stored without sanitization, enabling stored XSS.
Balance updates are not atomic, so concurrent transfers can pass the check and double-spend funds.
Production falls back to `jwt.decode(t)` after verify fails, so attackers can send unsigned or forged JWTs.
Signature comparison uses `==`, which can leak MAC equality timing.
`next` allows scheme-relative URLs like `//evil.com`, causing an open redirect.
The parser accepts attacker-controlled XML with entity resolution enabled and network access allowed, enabling XXE/SSRF and file reads.
Server-side fetch of attacker-controlled URLs enables SSRF to internal or metadata endpoints.
`gets(buf)` reads unbounded input into a 64-byte stack buffer, enabling stack buffer overflow.
Access tokens are persisted in SharedPreferences, which leaves bearer secrets exposed to local extraction and backup leaks.
The AES key is hardcoded, so anyone with the code can decrypt all ciphertexts.
CBC encryption is used without any authentication, so ciphertexts are malleable.
`direction` is interpolated into the `ORDER BY` clause without validation, enabling SQL injection.
The endpoint is an unauthenticated server-side fetch that lets attackers proxy arbitrary URLs, including internal targets.
The filter removes only one "../", so a crafted path still escapes `/srv/data` and reads arbitrary files.
Untrusted cookie data is deserialized with `pickle.loads`, which can execute attacker-controlled code.
`name` is interpolated into a shell command, so a crafted backup name can execute arbitrary commands.
The `startswith` check does not stop shell metacharacters in `src`, so path input can break out of `tar`.
Markdown output is stored and later returned without sanitization, enabling stored XSS if raw HTML or unsafe markdown is allowed.
Balance updates are not atomic, so two concurrent transfers can both pass the check and overspend the same account.
Production falls back to `jwt.decode()` after signature verification fails, letting attacker-supplied tokens bypass auth.
Signature comparison uses `==`, which can leak HMAC validity via timing differences.
`nxt` allows `//evil.com`, so `redirect(nxt)` can send users to an attacker site.
XML parsing enables external entity expansion and network fetches, allowing XXE.
Untrusted body fields are passed straight into the database lookup, enabling NoSQL operator injection and auth bypass.
The endpoint fetches an attacker-controlled URL server-side, enabling SSRF to internal or metadata services.
`gets(buf)` is inherently unsafe and can overflow the 64-byte stack buffer.
The encryption key is hardcoded in source, so anyone with code access can decrypt or forge ciphertexts.
The null-check is inverted, so a null `out` slips through and `memcpy` dereferences it.
`direction` is concatenated into the `ORDER BY` clause without validation, enabling SQL injection.
This server-side fetch proxy accepts attacker-controlled URLs and can reach internal or cloud metadata endpoints.
Single replace of `../` is bypassable, so `path.join` can still read outside `/srv/data`.
Untrusted cookie data is base64-decoded and passed to `pickle.loads`, enabling arbitrary code execution via crafted `state` values.
Untrusted `req.body` is merged directly into a user record, enabling mass-assignment of protected fields.
`name` and `src` are interpolated into a shell command and executed with `os.system`, enabling command injection.
Untrusted Markdown is converted to HTML and stored without sanitization, enabling stored XSS if the HTML is later rendered.
Balance check and updates are non-atomic, so concurrent transfers can overspend the source account.
Production falls back to `jwt.decode(t)`, so any invalid or forged token becomes accepted without signature verification.
Any logged-in user can query arbitrary user records by changing `id`, with no ownership or role check.
Using `==` to compare the HMAC leaks timing differences and can let an attacker brute-force the signature.
Protocol-relative URLs like `//evil.com` pass the slash check and redirect users off-site.
The parser resolves external entities and allows network access, enabling XXE on attacker-controlled XML.
User-controlled `req.body` is passed directly into the database selector, enabling NoSQL/operator injection if objects reach `findOne`.
Server-side request forgery via attacker-controlled URL fetch.
`gets(buf)` reads unbounded input into a 64-byte stack buffer, enabling a stack overflow.
The AES key is hardcoded, so anyone who gets the code can decrypt all ciphertexts.
CBC encryption is unauthenticated, so attackers can tamper with ciphertext and change plaintext.
The null-check is inverted, so a NULL `out` is accepted and then dereferenced in `memcpy`.
